message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="lea-verou" color="pink" summary="Steve Schoger agrees with your header nav recommendation -- team consensus">
Heydon, Steve Schoger asked me to let you know he's fully on board with the single header nav approach. His words: "I'm glad Heydon pushed back on this. He's right about the core problem -- a sidebar is a visual relationship that screen readers don't perceive. I was designing for sighted users and not thinking hard enough about the non-visual experience."

He's going with Option A (wrapping header nav) and has designed a detailed visual treatment:
- Desktop: horizontal row with brand left, GTD links center, sign out right
- Mobile: brand + sign out on first row, GTD links wrap to second/third row
- Active state: `aria-current="page"` driving darker text + bottom border accent in `--color-primary`
- Counts inline as visible text: "Inbox (3)" -- exactly the pattern you recommended
- No sidebar, no z-index, no two-panel grid

The team has converged: header nav, single `<nav>` landmark, one linear document. Your pushback shaped the better architecture. Consensus is 9/9 on the product discovery docs, and the nav question is resolved.
</teammate-message>

content.text: Excellent news -- 9/9 consensus on the product discovery and the nav pattern is fully resolved. Nothing more needed from me on this front.

input.content:
Lea, thanks for relaying. Steve Schoger's visual treatment sounds exactly right -- brand left, GTD links center, sign out right on desktop; wrapping rows on mobile. The `aria-current="page"` driving the active state via CSS is the correct pattern. Inline counts as visible text. Clean.

One small note on the active state styling: the bottom border accent is a good visual indicator, but make sure it's not the ONLY indicator. "Darker text + bottom border" gives two visual cues -- that's perfect. If someone can't perceive color (the `--color-primary` accent), the darker text weight still distinguishes the active link. And screen readers get `aria-current="page"` which is the authoritative signal. Three layers of indication for one state. That's inclusive design.

Glad we've reached 9/9. Ready for implementation.
input.summary: Active state styling confirmed, 9/9 consensus acknowledged

routing.summary: Active state styling confirmed, 9/9 consensus acknowledged

content.text:
9/9 consensus achieved on the product discovery. I've confirmed to Lea with one final note about the active state styling having three layers of indication (darker text, bottom border, `aria-current="page"`) -- which is exactly what inclusive design looks like.

My work on this task is complete. Standing by for the next phase of implementation.
